model_findings.description: `ab serve --openai --host 0.0.0.0` exposes an unauthenticated proxy that can launch local agent CLIs using the user's environment and credentials. For a local-only bridge, non-loopback binding should require an explicit opt-in such as `--allow-remote` or be rejected entirely.

`GET /healthz` runs every backend probe on each request, which forks four subprocesses with one-second timeouts. An unauthenticated health endpoint is commonly polled and easy to hit repeatedly, so this creates an avoidable process-spawn denial-of-service surface; cache probe results or make healthz a cheap in-process check.

`/v1/chat/completions` accepts arbitrary-size message bodies, flattens the full request into one prompt, and immediately launches a backend process with a 120-second timeout and no concurrency or payload limit. A few large or parallel requests can consume memory, saturate subprocess slots, and tie up the service; enforce request size/message limits and add backpressure around backend execution.

Gemini, Claude, and Hermes prompts are passed as command-line arguments (`--prompt=...`, positional prompt, and `--oneshot=...`). User prompts may contain secrets and can be visible to other local processes through process listings while the backend runs, and large prompts can also hit OS argument-size limits; pass prompt content via stdin or a protected temporary file instead.

The request model allows extra OpenAI fields, but the endpoint does not reject or implement `stream=true`. OpenAI-compatible clients that request streaming will receive a normal JSON completion instead of an SSE stream, which can break client parsers; explicitly reject unsupported streaming requests or implement the streaming response shape.
